g.data-narration: Welcome to Distributed Systems - a comprehensive topic covering networked computing architectures.
A distributed system is a collection of networked computers that work together to provide shared resources and services.
g.text: A collection of physically separate, possibly heterogeneous computer systems that are networked
to provide users with access to various resources the system maintains.
Benefits: Increased computation speed, functionality, data availability, and reliability

g.data-narration: Four key motivations drive distributed systems: resource sharing, computation speedup, reliability, and communication.
Networks vary by scope - LAN covers a building, WAN spans cities, MAN covers a city, PAN connects personal devices.
A Network Operating System enables remote resource access through remote login and file transfer, with each machine running its own OS.
g.text: Provides environment for accessing remote resources via:
Each machine has its own OS

g.data-narration: In a Network OS, users must manually specify which machine to connect to - there's no transparency between systems.
g.text: Users must know which machine has what resources

g.data-narration: A Distributed OS provides transparency - users see a single system while the OS handles data migration, computation migration, and process migration.
g.text: Users see ONE unified system - OS handles everything transparently:

g.data-narration: In a Distributed OS, machines share a single distributed OS layer that handles resource location and management transparently.
g.text: Users are unaware of multiple machines - seamless experience

g.data-narration: Key difference: Network OS makes users aware of multiple machines while Distributed OS provides complete transparency.
The client-server model has servers providing services and clients requesting them - the most common distributed system structure.
In P2P, all nodes are equal - each acts as both client and server. No central server means no single point of failure.
Clustered systems couple multiple CPUs for high availability and performance - asymmetric has hot standby, symmetric has all active nodes.
g.text: Used for High Performance Computing (HPC) - parallelized applications across nodes

g.data-narration: Key concepts: distributed systems enable resource sharing, with Network OS requiring manual access and Distributed OS providing transparency.
Exam tips: Focus on Network OS vs Distributed OS differences, client-server vs P2P architectures, and clustering types.
g.text: 1. Network OS vs Distributed OS - Key difference is TRANSPARENCY
2. Client-server vs P2P - Know architectures, advantages, disadvantages
3. Asymmetric (hot standby) vs Symmetric (both active) clustering
Draw diagrams in exams - they help explain concepts!
